snippet.title: Путин предупреждал! И в 2022 были учения! Россияне о новых учениях и угрозах. Опрос, Москва
snippet.description:
Министерство обороны Беларуси в понедельник, 18 мая, объявило о начале учений подразделений боевого применения ядерного оружия и ядерного обеспечения, проводимых совместно с российской стороной. В их ходе планируется отработать вопросы доставки ядерных боеприпасов и подготовки их к применению. Среди заявленных целей - проверка готовности техники и вооружений, а также отработка боевого применения из незапланированных районов дислокации.

Основное внимание на учениях уделяется скрытности передвижения, маршам на значительные расстояния и проведению расчетов для применения сил и средств. Минобороны Беларуси подчеркнуло, что мероприятия носят плановый характер и не направлены против третьих стран.

snippet.title: General Medicine HA License | Common Conditions & Diagnostic Procedures
snippet.description:
The General Medicine HA License Class by Smart Academy is designed for students preparing for the Health Assistant (HA) License Examination. This class focuses on common medical conditions, diagnosis, treatment protocols, and patient care, equipping students with exam-oriented knowledge and practical strategies to confidently succeed in the HA license exam.

Topics Covered:
• Fundamentals of General Medicine
• Common Medical Conditions and Their Management
• Diagnostic Procedures and Patient Assessment
• Treatment Protocols and Care Guidelines
• HA License Exam Preparation Tips and Strategies

snippet.title: Linux Dumps Ham Radio, Public Safety License Record, And Hamvention Is Finally Over
snippet.description:
00:00 Moar LMR & Public Safety Licenses
00:20 Linux Drops Amateur Radio Support 
00:52 Rapid Radios Are Legal
01:50 Hamvention 2026
02:04 Cobra Trail Blazer 450 Roger Beep
In This weeks Radio Dork news:
According to FCC data, last year over 3,500 LMR Business and Public Safety license applications were submitted to the FCC and over 3,400 were granted by the FCC, making 2025 the busiest year for Business and LMR licenses in the last 9 years.

Linux has removed the very popular AX.25 subsystem and related amateur radio protocol & drivers from the Linux kernel. The low-level kernel ham radio AX.25 subsystem, which has not been updated in over 6 years was removed due to a lack of maintainers and a large increase in bug reports. By removing AX.25 from the kernel, Linux software developers will now have to implement their own user-level AX.25 stack or make use of existing Python libraries.

The National Advertising division of the Better Business Bureau has decreed that Rapid Radios may continue to make advertising claims that its My Emergency Radio is “Legal to Own with no FCC License Required.”  The National Advertising division of the Better Business Bureau has determined that the claim of "no license required to own" was accurate under FCC regulations because according to FCC regulations, no license is needed to operate the device during an actual emergency. As part of the resolution, Rapid Radios agreed to comply with an Advertising and Voluntary Compliance agreement with the Michigan State Attorney General’s Office that requires clear disclosures about relevant FCC usage requirements, and the company has also permanently discontinued several other challenged advertising claims related to its products.

And finally, what is likely the biggest news in the GMRS world in the last year, hamvention is finally over.

And that is all of the radio-dork news that is fit to print. Join me again next week, or sooner, when I may, or may not, do this again.

snippet.title: SOTA at Mt Stokes
snippet.description:
SOTA at Mt Stokes
Mt Stokes is the highest point in the Marlborough Sounds - its about a 2.5 hour drive from Blenheim along a very narrow road followed by a 2 hour scramble up the mountain. I’ve been wanting to come here for some time so I’m very pleased that it seems that the weather has finally turned a corner and it looks like we are in for a fantastic day.
The summit has been activated before and also houses repeaters for Mt Campbell Communications and a few other organisations. The view is amazing and well worth the effort. Let’s hope that the bands are also in good condition today!

00:29 Mt Stokes Introduction
03:00 Activating
13:26 Wrap-up & Equipment
17:49 At the summit





I purchased my PMR-171 and Spiderbeam mast from TheHamShack: https://thehamshack.co.nz
I purchased my IC-705 from RWB: https://rwb.co.nz/product-category/amateur/
I purchased my FX-4CR from: https://bg2fx.com/transceivers-1

snippet.title: FCC Citation For Unlicensed Operation, Ted Cruz Thanks Ham Radio Operators & FCC Roundup For May
snippet.description:
00:00 Thanks for your Amateur Radio Service
00:29 Busted!
01:28 FCC Enforcement Roundup
02:44 Retevis RB48 Pro Roger Beep
United States Senator Ted Cruz has released a Memorial Day message praising brave ham radio heroes for their public service in recent disasters in the United States, highlighting amateur radio operator's role and importance when power and cellular networks fail.  Senator Ted Cruz went on to express his personal thanks to these brave first-responders for their courage and commitment, and pointed out that Amateur Radio strengthens our communities.

The FCC has sent a Notice of Unauthorized Authorization, often referred to as a mean letter, to a man in Bell California for transmitting on the ham radio 2Meter band without an amateur radio license, exceeding permitted power-levels and causing harmful interference. In March of this year after receiving multiple complaints about unlicensed transmissions and harmful interference in the Southern California area, an FCC agent used direction finding techniques and located a home on Palm Avenue in the city of Bell California, where the agent then observed transmissions on 146.415Mhz coming from said home, including statements transmitted over the air proclaiming that "Jack is back". The unlicensed operator now has 10 days to respond in writing to the FCC to explain why he, a grown man, was being a dickhead on his ham radio.

And now for this month's FCC enforcement roundup. Here is the breakdown of what the FCC got up to in the month of May, 2026. As reported a moment ago, the FCC sent a mean letter to a man in Southern California for  transmitting on ham radio frequencies without an amateur radio license, exceeding permitted power-levels and causing harmful interference.
The FCC modified one commercial radio station licenses.
The FCC sent violation notices to 6 commercial radio stations.
They agreed to an $80,000 settlement with a phone company for providing phone service without FCC authorization.
They issued a fine of $14,000 to a New York broadband provider for failing to adhere to oversight and anti-fraud regulations.
They published their list of criminal regulatory offenses that the agency has the power to enforce, as directed by Presidential Executive Order 14294,
and now the part you've all been waiting for:  the total number of jail-time sentences or fines issued to CB radio, GMRS, or amateur radio operators was: ZERO
and that's the way it is. Join me again next week.. or sooner, when I may, or may not, do this again.

snippet.title: California DMV Technician Indicted in Driver's License Fraud Ring With 14 Others
snippet.description:
In 2014, Alfonso Casarez — a Senior Vehicle Technician at the California Department of Motor Vehicles field office in Fresno, California — was indicted on 34 federal counts including criminal conspiracy under 18 U.S.C. § 371, unlawful production of identification documents, and unlawful transfer of identification documents after a joint investigation by Homeland Security Investigations and the California DMV's own Investigations Division revealed he had electronically falsified DMV test records to issue fraudulent Class C and Class A commercial driver's licenses to applicants who had never passed the required written or behind-the-wheel examinations. Operating as the inside anchor of a three-role fraud ring, Casarez received cash payments routed through broker Manjit Singh and primary recruiter Yadwinder Singh, then entered false passing scores into the DMV's electronic database, causing the state system to generate authentic California driver's licenses for unqualified recipients. The scheme targeted Central Valley trucking-community members facing language barriers to legitimate commercial licensing. The investigation was triggered by statistical anomalies in Casarez's test-entry records identified by the DMV's own Investigations Division. Fourteen of the fifteen indicted defendants were arrested in a coordinated takedown following the unsealing of two parallel indictments in the Eastern District of California. Among the licenses fraudulently issued were Class A commercial driver's licenses authorizing holders to operate tractor-trailer combinations weighing up to 80,000 pounds — vehicles whose federal testing requirements exist precisely because of catastrophic public-safety consequences when unqualified drivers operate them on American highways.

- - -
Disclaimer: This video is a dramatization based on real events. Some visual content was created with artificial intelligence assistance. Some details have been fictionalized and all names have been changed to protect the privacy of those involved.

snippet.title: The Ham Radio: Where Did 750,000 Operators Go?
snippet.description:
This is the full story — from the spark gap transmitters of 1900, through two world wars, through the golden age of the 1950s and 60s, through the demographic turn nobody wanted to admit, all the way to Hurricane Helene in 2024, when ham radio was the only communication system still working in western North Carolina.
The frequencies are still allocated. The equipment still exists. The question is whether the people who know how to use it will still be there when they're needed.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

snippet.title: From SSB to CW: A Conversation with Brian, WQ0A, on the Morse Code Side of Parks On The Air
snippet.description:
When you spend enough time on the Parks On The Air (POTA) bands, you start to notice that there are two distinct communities operating side-by-side: the SSB crowd and the CW crowd. They share the same parks, chase the same activations, and celebrate the same milestones — but the way they go about it can look surprisingly different. To get some insight into that contrast, I sat down with Brian, WQ0A, a Kansas City–area ham who has experienced POTA from both sides of the mode divide.

A Little About Brian

Brian hails from the Kansas City metro area, where he lives with his wife and three kids. His career has been defined by service. He spent four years in the United States Air Force, from 1996 to 2000, working as a firefighter. From there, he made a lateral transfer to the Lenexa Fire Department, just outside Kansas City, where he served for 25 years before recently retiring. By buying back his military time, Brian wrapped up his career with a rounded 30 years in fire service — an impressive run by any measure.

Now retired, Brian has more time than ever to enjoy one of his favorite pursuits: getting out into the field and activating parks with a radio and a key.

Starting on SSB

Like a lot of POTA operators, Brian started his activating journey on single sideband. SSB is the natural entry point for many activators — it's fast to set up, you can chat with hunters, and the contacts feel immediate and personal. Brian enjoyed his time on phone, but as he listened around the bands and watched the spots roll in, the rhythmic cadence of CW kept catching his ear.

Eventually, curiosity got the better of him, and he decided to jump into the Morse Code side of POTA.

A Different Pace, A Different Culture

According to Brian, CW activators tend to do things a little differently than their SSB counterparts. SSB activations are often louder, busier, and more conversational — pileups can get chaotic, signal reports get exchanged quickly, and hunters frequently want to chat for a moment before moving on. CW activations, by contrast, have a more measured rhythm. Exchanges are typically shorter and more standardized: a call sign, a signal report, and you're on to the next contact. There's a quiet efficiency to it.

That structure, Brian says, is part of what drew him in. CW lets you move through a pileup methodically, and there's a meditative quality to sitting in a park, listening to dits and dahs, and working stations one at a time. It feels less like a contest and more like a craft.

A Welcoming Community

One of the things Brian was most eager to share was how welcoming the CW POTA community has been. There's a stereotype that CW operators can be gruff or impatient with newcomers, but Brian's experience has been the opposite. When he started activating on CW, hunters slowed down for him, sent at speeds he could copy, and were patient when he had to ask for repeats. Many sent encouraging messages afterward, and a few reached out directly to congratulate him on stepping into the mode.

That kind of mentorship and support, he says, made all the difference. It turned what could have been an intimidating leap into an enjoyable transition.

Why He Stays on CW

These days, Brian spends most of his POTA time on CW. He still appreciates SSB and the friends he made there, but the CW side has captured something he didn't realize he was looking for: a slower, more focused way to enjoy the hobby, paired with a community that genuinely roots for one another.

For anyone on the fence about trying CW for POTA, Brian's advice is simple: jump in. The community will meet you where you are, and the rewards — both on the air and off — are worth every dit and dah.

73, Brian, WQ0A — and thanks for sharing your story.

#POTA, #HamRadio, #CW, #MorseCode, #ParksOnTheAir, #AmateurRadio, #POTACW, #HamLife, #QRP, #HamRadioOperator

snippet.title: 15 m #hamradio  Band
snippet.description:
The 15 metre band is one of the most popular HF amateur radio bands for long-distance communication, offering an excellent balance between reliability and worldwide reach.

The 15 metre band works primarily through ionospheric propagation, specifically via the F layer of the ionosphere. Signals transmitted on 21 MHz travel upward and are refracted back to Earth, allowing DX (long-distance communication) over thousands of kilometres. This band is highly dependent on solar activity, especially the sunspot cycle. During periods of high solar activity, the maximum usable frequency (MUF) increases, allowing 15m signals to propagate globally with strong signals. During low solar activity, the band can appear quiet or “closed,” especially for long-distance paths.

Operators can expect fast-changing propagation conditions on 15 metres. When the band is open, signals are often strong, with low noise compared to lower HF bands like 40m or 80m. This makes it ideal for DX contacts, contesting, and digital modes. However, when the band is closed, there may be little to no activity except for occasional short skip (regional contacts) or sporadic openings. Unlike lower bands, 15m does not usually support reliable nighttime propagation, as the ionosphere loses its ability to refract higher frequencies after sunset.

The interaction with ionospheric layers is key to understanding 15m performance. The F1 and F2 layers are responsible for most long-distance propagation, particularly during the daytime. The D layer, which forms during daylight hours, can absorb lower-frequency signals but has less effect on 15m, helping keep noise levels lower. Occasionally, sporadic E propagation can also open the band, especially in summer, allowing unexpected medium-distance contacts even when the F layer is weak.

See more on my blog. 

Don't forget to see my other videos on this.

snippet.title: Ham Radio vs GMRS: A Reality Check
snippet.description:
In this video, we break down the misleading claims from "When Facts Hurt Feelings - The GMRS vs. Ham Reality Check" and set the record straight on GMRS vs Amateur Radio for real-world preparedness.
From exaggerated FCC data interpretations and family licensing myths to emergency response realities (including Hurricane Helene), we examine the evidence, technical limitations, and why ham radio's capabilities, range, power, digital modes, and global reach remain unmatched for serious EmComm.

✅ What you'll learn:
The actual strengths and weaknesses of GMRS for families and local use
Why ham radio's licensing, frequency access, and infrastructure provide superior options in disasters
Fact vs. hype: repeater access, power limits, interoperability, and public safety parallels
Balanced view: When GMRS makes sense alongside ham radio (not instead of it)

snippet.title: 🔥 INSANE 10,418 Mile DX! YB8AO 🇮🇩 ↔ W4KWS 🇺🇸 | 20M Long Path Magic!
snippet.description:
What an incredible DX contact! 🌍
In this video, W4KWS (Key West, Florida) connects with YB8AO (Indonesia) on the 20 meter band (14.252 MHz USB) covering a massive 16,767 km (10,418 miles) using long path propagation.

This is what ham radio is all about — real RF, real propagation, and global communication! 🌎📡

🌐 📡 PROPAGATION BREAKDOWN (DETAILED)

This contact was achieved via LONG PATH propagation, meaning the signal traveled the opposite direction around the Earth — the long way around 🌍➡️🌏

Key propagation factors that made this possible:

☀️ F-Layer Ionospheric Reflection

The F2 layer supported sustained HF propagation over extreme distances
Likely enhanced by elevated solar activity and stable ionization levels

📶 20 Meter Band Sweet Spot

20m (14 MHz) is one of the most reliable DX bands
Supports worldwide propagation during both day and greyline transitions

🌅 Grayline Enhancement

Signals likely benefited from sunrise/sunset overlap between regions
Reduced D-layer absorption allowed signals to travel further with less loss

🌊 Ocean Path Advantage

Signal path traveled largely over saltwater, improving conductivity
Lower noise floor = stronger, cleaner signals

📡 Stable Conditions

Minimal fading and consistent signal strength
Achieved RST 57 / 57 both ways — solid copy across the globe

This is a textbook example of how propagation knowledge + timing = DX success 💪

📡 W4KWS STATION (Key West, FL USA)

👤 Operator: Tony Garcia
📍 Location: Key West, Florida
📡 Grid: EL94cn
🌎 IOTA: NA-062
🌐 ITU Zone: 8 | CQ Zone: 4

Station Equipment:

🎙️ Transceiver: Icom IC-7300 
⚡ Amplifier: RF-POWER RF2K-S HF Solid State Linear
Automatic band switching
Built-in protection systems
Network control capability
📡 Antenna: HexBeam @ ~20 ft AGL
Directional multi-band HF antenna
Excellent front-to-back performance
Low noise reception — ideal for DX
🌏 DX STATION (YB8AO – Indonesia)

👤 Operator: Andi M. Yauri
📍 Watampone, Bone, South Sulawesi
📡 Grid: PI05d1
🌎 IOTA: OC-146
🌐 ITU Zone: 54 | CQ Zone: 28

🔗 STATION LINKS (QRZ)

👉 https://www.qrz.com/db/W4KWS
👉 https://www.qrz.com/db/YB8AO

💥 WHY THIS CONTACT IS AWESOME

🚀 10,000+ mile contact
📡 Low antenna height (~20 ft!)
🌍 Long path propagation
📶 Strong 57/57 signal both ways

This proves you don’t need massive towers to work serious DX — just the right setup, timing, and understanding of propagation.

👍 SUPPORT THE CHANNEL

If you enjoy DX contacts, ham radio setups, and real on-air action:

👍 Like
🔔 Subscribe
📤 Share

73 and see you on the bands!
— W4KWS

snippet.title: A DIGIPEATER??!  Best Handheld Radio for Beginner APRS Learners!
snippet.description:
I'm pretty impressed with this 10W handheld radio from @tidradio  actually.  I was reluctant since I had no idea about APRS and SMS messaging on Radios but this Radio was very easy to learn on with those features and now it's my daily carry!  Be sure to have the following versions:

RADIO INFO:
Firmware Version:  V1.0.32
Hardware Version: V1.0.1
APRS INFO:
Firmware Version:  V1.0.15
Hardware Version:  V1.0.0

Tidradio TD-H9 (BLACK):  https://amzn.to/4nZETgX
Tidradio TD-H9  (ORANGE):  https://amzn.to/49vimma
Tidradio TD-H9 (CRYSTAL):  https://amzn.to/4dDVpzM

Some Morse Code Instructions:
Set both TD-H9 walkie-talkies to the same channel frequency for normal communication. Enter the menu to turn on the Morse code switch. On one device, use key transmission or edit content to send Morse codes. On the other device, enter the decoding menu to receive and decode signals.

IMPORTANT NOTE: Both devices must be set to the same transmission speed. If decoding fails, adjust the receiving threshold.

Detailed Menu Instructions for Morse Code
1.  Morse Code Switch:  Master switch to enable or disable the Morse code function.
2.  Key Transmission Mode:  Send standard real-time Morse codes via wireless signal by pressing PTT key:

Operation: Tap PTT to input . or —
Short press means  Dot (·)
Long press means  Dash (—)

3.  Edit & Send Mode:  Pre-edit text content and send all Morse codes with one click after confirmation.
Operation: Default text is "HELLO" in the input box; enter desired text via numeric keypad.

Press left key for submenu:
Send: Transmit codes at current WPM speed with progress display; press BACK to stop midway
Clear: Empty all input content

4. RX Decoding Mode:  Monitor and decode incoming Morse signals in real time, decoded text will display on screen word by word.
Left OK key: Clear cache and restart reception
Right exit key: Quit decoding mode and stop receiving

5.  Transmission Speed (WPM):  Options: 5 / 8 / 12 (default) / 18 / 24 WPM
Higher WPM means faster speed. This setting applies to both sending and receiving.

6. Receiving Threshold:  Options: Low (Sensitive) / Medium-Low / Medium(default) / Medium-High / High
Raise threshold in strong signal areas to avoid noise interference; lower it for weak signal reception.

7.  Tone Frequency: Options: 500 / 600 / 700(default) / 800 / 1000 Hz
Determines the sidetone sound and modulated tone frequency of transmitted Morse codes.

0:00 Intro
0:46 Radio General things
1:42 GPS Set Up (GNSS)
3:44 APRS Settings/Beacon Set
7:16 APRS Beacon Type
8:10 APRS Relay Set (DIGIPEATER)
10:17 APRS Advanced Set
11:05 APRS Beacon List
11:37 APRS Frequency Set Up 144.390MHz
12:40 Morse Code
19:05 SMS over RF, Radio to Radio
22:15 SMS Radio to Cell Phone
28:49 Final Thoughts

snippet.title: Is There a MAGIC Antenna for POTA? The Honest Answer
snippet.description:
If there's one question I get asked more than any other in the Parks On The Air (POTA) community, it's this: "What's the magical antenna for POTA?" Hams new to portable operations are always on the hunt for that one secret weapon, the perfect setup that will guarantee successful activations every time.

I hate to be the bearer of disappointing news, but here's the honest truth: there is no magical antenna for POTA. No such thing exists. And once you accept that, you'll actually become a much better portable operator.

The Truth About Antennas and POTA

Any antenna will work well — with the right conditions, at the right time of day, on the right band. That's the real secret, and it has nothing to do with the antenna itself and everything to do with understanding propagation, band conditions, and how to match your equipment to the situation in front of you.

Over my time activating parks, I've had tremendous success with a variety of antennas, including:

End-Fed Half-Wave (EFHW) — Arguably the most popular POTA antenna for good reason. It's easy to deploy, multi-band capable, and works great when you can get one end up high in a tree.

KJ6ER PERformer — A solid performer (pun intended) for portable work that has earned its place in my rotation.

Wire Dipoles — The classic. Simple, efficient, and incredibly effective when deployed properly.

And plenty more along the way.

Each one has delivered successful activations. Each one has also had days where the bands just weren't cooperating, no matter how perfect the setup looked on paper.

What Actually Matters

If there's no magic antenna, what should you focus on? Here are the things that genuinely make or break a portable activation:

1. The Right Band for the Right Time of Day
This is the single most important concept to grasp. 40 meters at noon behaves very differently than 40 meters at sunset. 20 meters can be wide open during the day and dead at night. 15 and 10 meters can produce magic during good solar conditions but seem nonexistent during a solar minimum.

Learn the basics of propagation. Check the solar flux index, the A and K indices, and use tools like PSK Reporter or the Reverse Beacon Network to see what bands are actually open before you commit.

2. The Right Location
Your antenna can only do so much if you're set up in a deep valley surrounded by power lines and RFI. Find quiet locations with a reasonable takeoff angle in the directions you want to work. Open areas, hilltops, and lakeshores are your friends.

3. Antenna Efficiency
This is absolutely critical for portable operations. When you're running 5-10 watts from a battery-powered radio, you can't afford to waste signal in lossy coax, poor connections, or compromised antenna designs. A full-size resonant antenna almost always beats a heavily-loaded compact antenna when efficiency matters.

Get that radiator as high as you reasonably can. Use a decent counterpoise or radial system if your antenna requires one. Pay attention to feedline losses.

My Honest Advice

Stop searching for the magic bullet. It doesn't exist, and chasing it will only frustrate you and empty your wallet.

Instead:

Experiment. Try different antennas in different environments. Learn what works for you and your style of operating.

Find what suits your park visits. Do you hike in deep? You need something lightweight and compact. Drive up to a picnic table? You can deploy something more elaborate.

Keep playing. That's really what portable ham radio is all about. The joy is in the journey, the experimentation, and the satisfaction of making contacts with simple gear in beautiful places.

The best antenna for POTA is the one you'll actually deploy, that's appropriate for your conditions, and that you understand well enough to use effectively. That might be an EFHW for you, a dipole for someone else, or a vertical for the next operator down the trail.

So get out there, try things, make mistakes, log contacts, and most importantly — have fun. That's the real magic of Parks On The Air.

73 and see you on the bands!

---

snippet.title: Forget Expensive Radios: Your First Ham Setup for Under $30
snippet.description:
Thinking about getting into ham radio but worried about the cost? Think again! You DON'T need a $1000 radio to get started. In this quick video, I'll show you the only radio you actually need, and it costs about 30 bucks.

This basic handheld radio is VHF/UHF, battery-powered, and fits right in your pocket. It'll let you hit local repeaters, monitor weather frequencies, and make your first contacts! Get ready to explore the world of amateur radio without breaking the bank.

#HamRadio #AmateurRadio #Baofeng #BeginnerHamRadio #AffordableRadio #Prepping #SurvivalRadio #VHF #UHF #RadioCommunication

Known for re-writing the rules of antenna physics, Dr. Jack breaks down how this incredibly small, low-profile vertical antenna delivers efficient, multi-band performance. snippet.title: Chasing His 6th Mountain Goat — Derek W7DLZ on SOTA & QRP
snippet.description:
Derek W7DLZ has earned the SOTA Mountain Goat award five times over and he's closing in on a sixth. Since his first activation in September 2021, Derek has operated from hundreds of summits across six associations and twenty-two regions at a pace that's hard to believe, and he shows no signs of slowing down.
In this episode we cover his preferred radio, antenna choices, and operating philosophy.
If you're into SOTA, QRP field ops, or just want to hear what genuine passion for a hobby sounds like, this one's for you.

https://www.qrz.com/db/W7DLZ

Join us as we explore how you can get involved in portable radio, QRP, and more in this episode of the All Portable Discussion Zone (AP/DZ). Every aspect of portable operations is covered in this biweekly podcast, from news and gear to achievements, the workbench, contests, awards, and beyond.

snippet.title: What's That Mysterious Chirp on the Ham Bands?
snippet.description:
If you've spent any time tuning around the HF amateur radio bands—especially 20 meters—you've almost certainly heard it: a rapid "chirp" that sweeps up through the band, often accompanied by a distinctive slanted line on your waterfall display. New hams sometimes wonder if their radio is broken or if there's interference from a nearby device. The answer is far more interesting. You're hearing an ionosonde.

What Is an Ionosonde?

An ionosonde, also called a chirpsounder, is a specialized radar designed to examine the ionosphere—the electrically charged layer of Earth's upper atmosphere that makes long-distance HF communication possible. Instead of tracking aircraft, an ionosonde points upward, bouncing signals off the ionospheric layers to determine which frequencies will support propagation at any given moment.

The technology was invented in 1925 by Gregory Breit and Merle A. Tuve and further developed in the late 1920s by physicists including Sir Edward Victor Appleton. The term "ionosphere" itself was coined by Robert Watson-Watt, one of the principal architects of radar.

How It Works

An ionosonde is a Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar consisting of an HF transmitter, a tracking receiver, a vertically-oriented antenna, and digital control circuits. The transmitter emits short pulses while sweeping across the HF spectrum—typically 1 to 40 MHz, though most operational sweeps focus on 1.6–12 MHz.

These pulses reflect off the D, E, F1, and F2 layers at altitudes of 100 to 400 km (62 to 249 miles). The receiver measures the time delay between transmission and reception, plotting the results on an ionogram—a graph of reflection height versus frequency.

Why That Distinctive Chirp?

The reason ionosondes sound like rapid chirps on amateur bands has everything to do with their sweep. As the ionosonde rapidly climbs from low to high frequency, it briefly passes through every HF amateur band along the way—including 80m, 40m, 20m, 15m, and 10m.

To a receiver tuned to a fixed frequency, this brief sweep manifests as:

An audible "chirrrp!" or "brrrrp!" lasting a fraction of a second
A characteristic slanted line on a waterfall display moving diagonally upward
Different ionosondes have different sweep rates and repetition intervals, giving them distinctive signatures experienced operators can often identify.

Practical Value for Hams

Ionosondes are legitimate, internationally coordinated scientific instruments. Their primary purpose is identifying optimum operating frequencies for HF communications by measuring parameters like the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) and Lowest Usable Frequency (LUF).

For amateur radio operators, hearing ionosondes is actually useful information. If you're receiving strong ionosonde signals, it means HF propagation is supporting signals from that ionosonde's location—suggesting favorable conditions for working that region. Many hams use freely available ionogram data from networks like the Global Ionospheric Radio Observatory (GIRO) to plan operating sessions based on real-time ionospheric measurements.

Conclusion

That mysterious chirp racing through 20 meters isn't a malfunction or interference—it's the sound of science in action. Each chirp represents an ionosonde probing the invisible layers above us, gathering data that helps amateur operators understand the dynamic environment that makes HF radio possible. So next time you hear that distinctive sweep, appreciate it for what it is: a brief auditory glimpse into a century-old technology that still plays a vital role in our hobby.
